title: Diabetes Insipidus
oneLiner: A disorder of water balance causing massive dilute urine output; think central (ADH lack) vs nephrogenic (kidney resistance).

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic Vignette:**  
A 28-year-old woman presents with 6 weeks of extreme thirst and waking 6 times per night to urinate. She has been drinking 8-10 litres of water daily. She is not diabetic, has no UTI symptoms, and urine dip is clear. She appears eunatremic and has no polyphagia or weight loss. She recently recovered from a viral meningoencephalitis.

**Dissect the vignette:**
- **Extreme thirst (polydipsia) + polyuria** → water diuresis, not osmotic diuresis (e.g. diabetes mellitus)
- **Waking multiple times (nocturia)** → loss of concentrating ability overnight
- **Normal blood glucose, no ketones** → rules out osmotic diuresis from hyperglycaemia
- **Recent meningoencephalitis** → potential hypothalamic/pituitary damage → central DI
- **EuNATREMIC** → patient still able to maintain normal sodium via adequate water intake (if they can keep up, Na is normal; if they can't, hypernatremia occurs)
- **Clear urine dip** → no glycosuria, no infection, no protein → pure water diuresis

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
- **Hypernatraemic dehydration in a frail elderly patient with dementia** → they cannot access water freely → polyuria underdiagnosed; presents with confusion, fever, hypernatremia → often mistaken for poor fluid intake.
- **Head trauma / post-neurosurgery patient** → sudden massive polyuria after pituitary surgery; urine output >200 ml/hr for consecutive hours → immediate concern for central DI.
- **Lithium-treated patient with gradual onset polyuria** → nephrogenic DI as a side effect; patient may not mention lithium unless asked.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**  
Diabetes insipidus (DI) is a condition where the kidneys cannot concentrate urine, producing large volumes of dilute urine (up to 15-20 L/day). The body loses water faster than it can be replaced, leading to severe thirst and, if untreated, dangerous hypernatremia and volume depletion.  

**Why it matters clinically:**  
- **Frequency:** Uncommon in general practice, but common in neurosurgical and neurocritical care units. Overlooked in patients with polydipsia who are assumed to have primary polydipsia (psychogenic).  
- **Stakes:** If untreated, severe hypernatremia can cause seizures, coma, and death. In post-operative patients, missed DI leads to catastrophic hypernatremia within hours.  
- **Common pitfall:** Mistaking polyuria for diabetes mellitus (urine glucose negative) or overdiuresis from loop diuretics. A 24-hour urine volume > 40 ml/kg is diagnostic of polyuria; then differentiate DI from primary polydipsia.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story:**  
Normal kidneys: water is reabsorbed in the collecting duct via aquaporin-2 channels, inserted in response to antidiuretic hormone (ADH, also called vasopressin). ADH is produced in the hypothalamus (supraoptic and paraventricular nuclei) and stored in the posterior pituitary. When plasma osmolality rises (e.g. dehydration), ADH is released, making urine concentrated.  
In DI, there is either **insufficient ADH** (central DI) or **renal resistance to ADH** (nephrogenic DI). Either way, water is not reabsorbed → dilute urine → water loss → rising plasma osmolality → thirst.

**Mechanism → sign:**
- **Polyuria (dilute urine, specific gravity <1.005, osmolality <300 mOsm/kg)** : No ADH effect → water stays in tubule → large volume.
- **Polydipsia** : Rising osmolality stimulates hypothalamic thirst centre (osmoreceptors). Patient drinks to defend plasma sodium.
- **Hypernatremia (if water intake inadequate)** : Net water loss → plasma sodium >145 mmol/L. Happens in unconscious patients, those with impaired thirst, or infants.
- **Nocturia** : Loss of normal circadian rhythm of ADH; or global inability to concentrate urine.
- **Normal heart rate/BP (usually)** : Pure water loss (hypotonic) does not deplete intravascular volume early; osmotic diuresis (e.g. glucose) does.

**Connect to pattern recognition:**  
The classic vignette patient has normal sodium because she drinks enough water. The post-operative head trauma patient cannot drink → hypernatremia. The lithium patient develops gradual polyuria because lithium accumulates in collecting duct cells, downregulating aquaporin-2 channels (resistance to ADH).
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem clue pointing to cause |
|----------|-------|-----------------------------|
| **Central DI** | | |
| Idiopathic | Most common (30-50%) | Gradual onset, no other findings; sometimes autoimmune (ADH antibodies) |
| Neurosurgical | Transphenoidal surgery, clipping of aneurysm | Polyuria immediately post-op; urine output >200 ml/hr for 2 consecutive hours |
| Traumatic brain injury | Basal skull fracture, hypothalamic injury | Head injury + polyuria + hypernatremia |
| Tumour | Craniopharyngioma, germinoma, metastases | Mass effect: headache, visual field defect, endocrine deficiencies |
| Infectious / inflammatory | Meningitis, encephalitis, sarcoidosis, Langerhans cell histiocytosis | Known infection/systemic inflammation + polyuria |
| Genetic | Autosomal dominant (AVP gene) | Family history; onset in childhood |
| **Nephrogenic DI** | | |
| Drugs | **Lithium** (most common cause) | Patient on lithium for bipolar disorder; may have tremor, lithium level check |
| Hypercalcemia | Malignancy, hyperparathyroidism | Polyuria + constipation + bone pain; serum Ca >2.6 mmol/L |
| Hypokalemia | Chronic diarrhea, diuretics | Low K+ on labs; weakness, cramps |
| Genetic | X-linked (AVPR2 gene) or autosomal recessive (AQP2) | Male infant with failure to thrive + hypernatremia + dilute urine |
| Renal disease | Pyelonephritis, obstructive uropathy, sickle cell nephropathy | History of recurrent UTIs, flank pain, or known sickle cell disease |
| **Commonest cause overall:** Lithium-induced nephrogenic DI in psychiatric populations; idiopathic central DI otherwise.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic approach:**  
1. Confirm polyuria: 24h urine volume > 40 ml/kg (e.g. >2.8 L for 70 kg person).  
2. Measure paired plasma and urine osmolality.

**Water deprivation test (gold standard):**  
- Withhold water for 8-18 hours (or until 5% weight loss). Monitor plasma osmolality, urine osmolality, weight, sodium.  
- **Normal response:** Urine osmolality > 800 mOsm/kg; ratio >2.  
- **Central DI:** Urine osmolality rises < 300 mOsm/kg (dilute) but then **rises >50% after desmopressin** (synthetic ADH).  
- **Nephrogenic DI:** Urine osmolality stays low and **does NOT respond to desmopressin** (rise <50%).  
- **Primary polydipsia (psychogenic):** Urine osmolality > 300 and continues to rise; plasma osmolality low-normal, sodium low-normal.  
- **Caution:** Do not start water deprivation if sodium >150 or if severe dehydration; may cause harm.

**Single best confirmatory finding:**  
- Central DI: polyuria + low urine osmolality (<300) + hypernatremia + ADH level low (<2 pg/ml) + rise in urine osmolality after desmopressin.  
- Nephrogenic DI: same but ADH level high (≥5 pg/ml) and no response to desmopressin.

**Other useful tests:**
- **Plasma osmolality:** If >295 mOsm/kg with dilute urine → DI (regardless of water deprivation).  
- **Sodium:** Hypernatremia suggests DI with inadequate water intake.  
- **Urine specific gravity:** <1.005 suggests DI (vs >1.025 for normal concentration).  
- **MRI brain:** Look for posterior pituitary bright spot loss (central DI).  
- **Serum calcium, potassium, lithium level** if nephrogenic cause suspected.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour):**  
- If patient has **hypernatremia (Na >150)** and polyuria:  
  - Calculate water deficit: Water deficit (L) = 0.6 * weight (kg) * (Na/140 - 1).  
  - Replace with **5% dextrose** or **0.45% saline** (if volume depleted) – never 0.9% saline (will worsen hypernatremia).  
  - **Do not correct too fast** – aim to lower Na by 0.5-1 mmol/L per hour; max 8-10 mmol/L in 24h to avoid cerebral oedema.  
  - For **central DI**, give **desmopressin 1-2 micrograms IV/IM/SC** (or 100-200 micrograms oral).  
  - For **nephrogenic DI**, desmopressin will not work; focus on water replacement and treat underlying cause (stop lithium, correct calcium, etc.).

**Ward (24-72 hours):**  
- Monitor hourly urine output, plasma sodium q4-6h.  
- Titrate desmopressin doses in central DI: start with 1-2mcg SC q12-24h, adjust to daily urine output <2.5 L and normal sodium.  
- In nephrogenic DI, if water intake still insufficient, consider **hydrochlorothiazide (HCTZ) 12.5-25 mg PO daily** – paradoxically reduces polyuria by inducing mild volume depletion → increased proximal tubule water reabsorption (caution: check potassium).  
- For lithium-induced DI, first stop lithium or reduce dose; might need **amiloride 5-10 mg daily** to block lithium entry into collecting duct cells.

**Long-term:**  
- Central DI: lifelong desmopressin maintenance (oral tablet 0.1-0.4 mg daily, titrated to thirst; or nasal spray 10-20 mcg).  
- Nephrogenic DI: avoid offending drugs, treat hypercalcemia/hypokalemia; if persistent, HCTZ ± amiloride.  
- Educate patient to drink to thirst, avoid excessive fluid intake when desmopressin is active (risk of hyponatremia).  

**What NOT to do:**  
- Do NOT use desmopressin for nephrogenic DI (no effect, may cause hyponatremia).  
- Do NOT give large boluses of hypotonic fluid to correct hypernatremia – gradual is safe.  
- Do NOT ignore polyuria after neurosurgery; it can be transient (triphasic response: DI → SIADH → DI again).  
- Do NOT assume hyponatremia is due to DI; in central DI overtreatment, desmopressin can cause water intoxication.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Diabetes Insipidus | Primary Polydipsia (Psychogenic) | Diabetes Mellitus (Type 1/2) | SIADH (Syndrome of Inappropriate ADH) |
|---------|-------------------|----------------------------------|-------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| **Polyuria** | Massive (>3-4 L/day) | Massive, can be >10 L/day | Moderate (osmotic diuresis) | None (oliguria in severe cases) |
| **Polydipsia** | Severe, driven by hyperosmolality | Severe, but driven by psychiatric cause | Moderate, linked to hyperglycemia | Absent (no thirst) |
| **Plasma sodium** | Normal (if drinking) or high | Low-normal to low (usually <135) | Normal or slightly high from osmotic diuresis | Low (<135) |
| **Plasma osmolality** | High-normal or high | Low (dilutional) | High (glucose contributes) | Low (hyponatremia) |
| **Urine osmolality** | Low (<300, often <200) | Low but rises with water deprivation | High (> plasma, due to glucose) | Inappropriately high (<100, but > plasma? Actually high relative to plasma) |
| **Blood glucose** | Normal | Normal | Elevated | Normal |
| **Response to water deprivation** | Urine osmolality stays low | Urine osmolality rises | Not indicated | Not applicable |
| **Key distinguishing test** | Desmopressin response (central) vs none (nephrogenic) | Low plasma osmolality, low sodium, no response to desmopressin needed | HbA1c ≥6.5%, hyperglycemia | High urine osmolality >100 mOsm/kg despite hyponatremia |

**Additional mimics:**
- **Overdiuresis due to loop diuretics** (check medication history, urine osmolality variable, high sodium and chloride in urine).
- **Osmotic diuresis from mannitol, glycerol, urea** (urine osmolality > 300, and measured plasma osmolality high because of osmoles).
- **Renal salt wasting** (polyuria + hyponatremia, but urine sodium >40 mmol/L, plasma urate low).
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Hypernatremic dehydration:**  
- *Presentation:* Confusion, lethargy, irritability, hyperreflexia, ataxia, seizures, coma.  
- *Key signs:* Dry mucous membranes, decreased skin turgor, tachycardia, hypotension (late).  
- *Action:* Correct slowly with 5% dextrose or free water via NG tube; monitor sodium q2-4h.  
- **Late presentation pattern:** Elderly nursing home resident with vague illness, found to have sodium 165, urine output not measured – common exam case.

**Seizures from rapid osmolar shifts:**  
- If hypernatremia corrected too quickly, water rushes into brain cells causing cerebral oedema → seizures, increased ICP.  
- *Rule:* Use hypotonic fluids at a rate to lower sodium by ≤0.5 mmol/L per hour.

**Chronic bladder enlargement / hydronephrosis** (rare but possible in long-standing severe polyuria if not recognized):  
- High urine volumes cause overdistension → poor bladder contractility, VUR.  
- *Prevention:* Keep urine volume <3 L/day with treatment.

**Desmopressin overdose → hyponatremia:**  
- Water retention without ADH regulation → fluid overload, hyponatremia → cerebral oedema.  
- *Monitor:* Regular sodium checks; educate patient to “skip a dose if you have no thirst or are drinking less than usual.”

**Thirst loss in central diabetes insipidus** (adipsic DI if hypothalamic osmoreceptors damaged):  
- Very dangerous: no thirst drive, severe hypernatremia develops despite polyuria. These patients require scheduled water intake + daily dosing.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonic: DI – "A Dilute Issue"**  
- **A** – ADH deficiency (central) or resistance (nephrogenic)  
- **D** – Dilute urine (low osmolality)  
- **I** – Inappropriate water loss  
- **S** – Sodium high (if inadequate intake) or normal (if drinking enough)

**Mnemonic: Cranial vs Renal – "Central gets DDAVP, Nephrogenic gets Nix"**  
- Central: Desmopressin works.  
- Nephrogenic: Nix the drug (e.g. lithium), Nix the hypercalcemia, Nix hypokalemia.

**5 high-yield pearls:**  
1. **Polyuria + sodium normal** → think primary polydipsia or DI with adequate drinking. Polyuria + sodium high → DI until proven otherwise.  
2. **Post-pituitary surgery: look for triphasic response** – early DI (0-2 days) → SIADH (2-10 days) → permanent DI. Never treat early DI aggressively because the second phase (SIADH) can cause hyponatremia if desmopressin is continued.  
3. **A single urine osmolality > 600 rules out DI** (except if the patient has early diabetes mellitus).  
4. **Hypercalcemia and hypokalemia both cause nephrogenic DI** – always check Ca and K in polyuric patient.  
5. **Desmopressin dose is not weight-based** – start low (1 mcg SC or 100 mcg PO) and titrate to urine output and thirst.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**  
- Mistaking polyuria from DI for diabetes mellitus – check blood glucose.  
- Giving large volumes of normal saline for hypernatremia – it's hypertonic relative to the patient; use hypotonic fluids.  
- Starting water deprivation without checking baseline sodium or clinical hydration status.  
- Forgetting that lithium can cause irreversible nephrogenic DI; early detection stops damage.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**

| Parameter | Threshold | Action |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| **Hypernatremia** | Na > 150 mmol/L (especially if acute) | Urgent senior review; correct slowly; consider ICU if symptoms (seizures, coma) |
| **Rising sodium > 10 mmol/L in 24h** | Rate of correction too rapid or ongoing water loss | Reassess fluid regimen; call senior |
| **Hypotension + tachycardia + poor urine output** | SBP < 90, HR > 120, urine <0.5 ml/kg/hr for 2h | Treat hypovolemia; escalate to ICU (may need central line, vassopressin if refractory) |
| **Seizures or GCS <8** | Any seizure or GCS drop | Crash call, IV lorazepam, urgent sodium; consider cerebral oedema |
| **NEWS2** | Score ≥5 | Escalate to registrar; ≥7 call critical care outreach |
| **Urine output >300 ml/hr for 2 consecutive hours in a patient with head trauma / neurosurgery** | High risk for central DI | Notify neurosurgery; start desmopressin after protocol; monitor sodium hourly |
| **Hyponatremia from desmopressin overdose** | Na <125 with confusion | Stop desmopressin; fluid restriction; consider hypertonic saline if seizures |

**In summary:** Rapidly worsening hypernatremia, neurological symptoms, and hemodynamic instability are red flags. In any polyuric patient with altered consciousness, prioritize checking sodium and starting correct treatment. Build a mental reflex: "Polyuria + high Na = water deprivation test and/or imaging – never just give desmopressin without confirming the type."

## Diabetes Insipidus
**One-liner:** A disorder of water balance causing massive dilute urine output; think central (ADH lack) vs nephrogenic (kidney resistance).

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic Vignette:**  
A 28-year-old woman presents with 6 weeks of extreme thirst and waking 6 times per night to urinate. She has been drinking 8-10 litres of water daily. She is not diabetic, has no UTI symptoms, and urine dip is clear. She appears eunatremic and has no polyphagia or weight loss. She recently recovered from a viral meningoencephalitis.

**Dissect the vignette:**
- **Extreme thirst (polydipsia) + polyuria** → water diuresis, not osmotic diuresis (e.g. diabetes mellitus)
- **Waking multiple times (nocturia)** → loss of concentrating ability overnight
- **Normal blood glucose, no ketones** → rules out osmotic diuresis from hyperglycaemia
- **Recent meningoencephalitis** → potential hypothalamic/pituitary damage → central DI
- **EuNATREMIC** → patient still able to maintain normal sodium via adequate water intake (if they can keep up, Na is normal; if they can't, hypernatremia occurs)
- **Clear urine dip** → no glycosuria, no infection, no protein → pure water diuresis

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
- **Hypernatraemic dehydration in a frail elderly patient with dementia** → they cannot access water freely → polyuria underdiagnosed; presents with confusion, fever, hypernatremia → often mistaken for poor fluid intake.
- **Head trauma / post-neurosurgery patient** → sudden massive polyuria after pituitary surgery; urine output >200 ml/hr for consecutive hours → immediate concern for central DI.
- **Lithium-treated patient with gradual onset polyuria** → nephrogenic DI as a side effect; patient may not mention lithium unless asked.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:**  
Diabetes insipidus (DI) is a condition where the kidneys cannot concentrate urine, producing large volumes of dilute urine (up to 15-20 L/day). The body loses water faster than it can be replaced, leading to severe thirst and, if untreated, dangerous hypernatremia and volume depletion.  

**Why it matters clinically:**  
- **Frequency:** Uncommon in general practice, but common in neurosurgical and neurocritical care units. Overlooked in patients with polydipsia who are assumed to have primary polydipsia (psychogenic).  
- **Stakes:** If untreated, severe hypernatremia can cause seizures, coma, and death. In post-operative patients, missed DI leads to catastrophic hypernatremia within hours.  
- **Common pitfall:** Mistaking polyuria for diabetes mellitus (urine glucose negative) or overdiuresis from loop diuretics. A 24-hour urine volume > 40 ml/kg is diagnostic of polyuria; then differentiate DI from primary polydipsia.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The story:**  
Normal kidneys: water is reabsorbed in the collecting duct via aquaporin-2 channels, inserted in response to antidiuretic hormone (ADH, also called vasopressin). ADH is produced in the hypothalamus (supraoptic and paraventricular nuclei) and stored in the posterior pituitary. When plasma osmolality rises (e.g. dehydration), ADH is released, making urine concentrated.  
In DI, there is either **insufficient ADH** (central DI) or **renal resistance to ADH** (nephrogenic DI). Either way, water is not reabsorbed → dilute urine → water loss → rising plasma osmolality → thirst.

**Mechanism → sign:**
- **Polyuria (dilute urine, specific gravity <1.005, osmolality <300 mOsm/kg)** : No ADH effect → water stays in tubule → large volume.
- **Polydipsia** : Rising osmolality stimulates hypothalamic thirst centre (osmoreceptors). Patient drinks to defend plasma sodium.
- **Hypernatremia (if water intake inadequate)** : Net water loss → plasma sodium >145 mmol/L. Happens in unconscious patients, those with impaired thirst, or infants.
- **Nocturia** : Loss of normal circadian rhythm of ADH; or global inability to concentrate urine.
- **Normal heart rate/BP (usually)** : Pure water loss (hypotonic) does not deplete intravascular volume early; osmotic diuresis (e.g. glucose) does.

**Connect to pattern recognition:**  
The classic vignette patient has normal sodium because she drinks enough water. The post-operative head trauma patient cannot drink → hypernatremia. The lithium patient develops gradual polyuria because lithium accumulates in collecting duct cells, downregulating aquaporin-2 channels (resistance to ADH).

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause | Stem clue pointing to cause |
|----------|-------|-----------------------------|
| **Central DI** | | |
| Idiopathic | Most common (30-50%) | Gradual onset, no other findings; sometimes autoimmune (ADH antibodies) |
| Neurosurgical | Transphenoidal surgery, clipping of aneurysm | Polyuria immediately post-op; urine output >200 ml/hr for 2 consecutive hours |
| Traumatic brain injury | Basal skull fracture, hypothalamic injury | Head injury + polyuria + hypernatremia |
| Tumour | Craniopharyngioma, germinoma, metastases | Mass effect: headache, visual field defect, endocrine deficiencies |
| Infectious / inflammatory | Meningitis, encephalitis, sarcoidosis, Langerhans cell histiocytosis | Known infection/systemic inflammation + polyuria |
| Genetic | Autosomal dominant (AVP gene) | Family history; onset in childhood |
| **Nephrogenic DI** | | |
| Drugs | **Lithium** (most common cause) | Patient on lithium for bipolar disorder; may have tremor, lithium level check |
| Hypercalcemia | Malignancy, hyperparathyroidism | Polyuria + constipation + bone pain; serum Ca >2.6 mmol/L |
| Hypokalemia | Chronic diarrhea, diuretics | Low K+ on labs; weakness, cramps |
| Genetic | X-linked (AVPR2 gene) or autosomal recessive (AQP2) | Male infant with failure to thrive + hypernatremia + dilute urine |
| Renal disease | Pyelonephritis, obstructive uropathy, sickle cell nephropathy | History of recurrent UTIs, flank pain, or known sickle cell disease |
| **Commonest cause overall:** Lithium-induced nephrogenic DI in psychiatric populations; idiopathic central DI otherwise.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic approach:**  
1. Confirm polyuria: 24h urine volume > 40 ml/kg (e.g. >2.8 L for 70 kg person).  
2. Measure paired plasma and urine osmolality.

**Water deprivation test (gold standard):**  
- Withhold water for 8-18 hours (or until 5% weight loss). Monitor plasma osmolality, urine osmolality, weight, sodium.  
- **Normal response:** Urine osmolality > 800 mOsm/kg; ratio >2.  
- **Central DI:** Urine osmolality rises < 300 mOsm/kg (dilute) but then **rises >50% after desmopressin** (synthetic ADH).  
- **Nephrogenic DI:** Urine osmolality stays low and **does NOT respond to desmopressin** (rise <50%).  
- **Primary polydipsia (psychogenic):** Urine osmolality > 300 and continues to rise; plasma osmolality low-normal, sodium low-normal.  
- **Caution:** Do not start water deprivation if sodium >150 or if severe dehydration; may cause harm.

**Single best confirmatory finding:**  
- Central DI: polyuria + low urine osmolality (<300) + hypernatremia + ADH level low (<2 pg/ml) + rise in urine osmolality after desmopressin.  
- Nephrogenic DI: same but ADH level high (≥5 pg/ml) and no response to desmopressin.

**Other useful tests:**
- **Plasma osmolality:** If >295 mOsm/kg with dilute urine → DI (regardless of water deprivation).  
- **Sodium:** Hypernatremia suggests DI with inadequate water intake.  
- **Urine specific gravity:** <1.005 suggests DI (vs >1.025 for normal concentration).  
- **MRI brain:** Look for posterior pituitary bright spot loss (central DI).  
- **Serum calcium, potassium, lithium level** if nephrogenic cause suspected.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour):**  
- If patient has **hypernatremia (Na >150)** and polyuria:  
  - Calculate water deficit: Water deficit (L) = 0.6 * weight (kg) * (Na/140 - 1).  
  - Replace with **5% dextrose** or **0.45% saline** (if volume depleted) – never 0.9% saline (will worsen hypernatremia).  
  - **Do not correct too fast** – aim to lower Na by 0.5-1 mmol/L per hour; max 8-10 mmol/L in 24h to avoid cerebral oedema.  
  - For **central DI**, give **desmopressin 1-2 micrograms IV/IM/SC** (or 100-200 micrograms oral).  
  - For **nephrogenic DI**, desmopressin will not work; focus on water replacement and treat underlying cause (stop lithium, correct calcium, etc.).

**Ward (24-72 hours):**  
- Monitor hourly urine output, plasma sodium q4-6h.  
- Titrate desmopressin doses in central DI: start with 1-2mcg SC q12-24h, adjust to daily urine output <2.5 L and normal sodium.  
- In nephrogenic DI, if water intake still insufficient, consider **hydrochlorothiazide (HCTZ) 12.5-25 mg PO daily** – paradoxically reduces polyuria by inducing mild volume depletion → increased proximal tubule water reabsorption (caution: check potassium).  
- For lithium-induced DI, first stop lithium or reduce dose; might need **amiloride 5-10 mg daily** to block lithium entry into collecting duct cells.

**Long-term:**  
- Central DI: lifelong desmopressin maintenance (oral tablet 0.1-0.4 mg daily, titrated to thirst; or nasal spray 10-20 mcg).  
- Nephrogenic DI: avoid offending drugs, treat hypercalcemia/hypokalemia; if persistent, HCTZ ± amiloride.  
- Educate patient to drink to thirst, avoid excessive fluid intake when desmopressin is active (risk of hyponatremia).  

**What NOT to do:**  
- Do NOT use desmopressin for nephrogenic DI (no effect, may cause hyponatremia).  
- Do NOT give large boluses of hypotonic fluid to correct hypernatremia – gradual is safe.  
- Do NOT ignore polyuria after neurosurgery; it can be transient (triphasic response: DI → SIADH → DI again).  
- Do NOT assume hyponatremia is due to DI; in central DI overtreatment, desmopressin can cause water intoxication.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Diabetes Insipidus | Primary Polydipsia (Psychogenic) | Diabetes Mellitus (Type 1/2) | SIADH (Syndrome of Inappropriate ADH) |
|---------|-------------------|----------------------------------|-------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| **Polyuria** | Massive (>3-4 L/day) | Massive, can be >10 L/day | Moderate (osmotic diuresis) | None (oliguria in severe cases) |
| **Polydipsia** | Severe, driven by hyperosmolality | Severe, but driven by psychiatric cause | Moderate, linked to hyperglycemia | Absent (no thirst) |
| **Plasma sodium** | Normal (if drinking) or high | Low-normal to low (usually <135) | Normal or slightly high from osmotic diuresis | Low (<135) |
| **Plasma osmolality** | High-normal or high | Low (dilutional) | High (glucose contributes) | Low (hyponatremia) |
| **Urine osmolality** | Low (<300, often <200) | Low but rises with water deprivation | High (> plasma, due to glucose) | Inappropriately high (<100, but > plasma? Actually high relative to plasma) |
| **Blood glucose** | Normal | Normal | Elevated | Normal |
| **Response to water deprivation** | Urine osmolality stays low | Urine osmolality rises | Not indicated | Not applicable |
| **Key distinguishing test** | Desmopressin response (central) vs none (nephrogenic) | Low plasma osmolality, low sodium, no response to desmopressin needed | HbA1c ≥6.5%, hyperglycemia | High urine osmolality >100 mOsm/kg despite hyponatremia |

**Additional mimics:**
- **Overdiuresis due to loop diuretics** (check medication history, urine osmolality variable, high sodium and chloride in urine).
- **Osmotic diuresis from mannitol, glycerol, urea** (urine osmolality > 300, and measured plasma osmolality high because of osmoles).
- **Renal salt wasting** (polyuria + hyponatremia, but urine sodium >40 mmol/L, plasma urate low).

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**Hypernatremic dehydration:**  
- *Presentation:* Confusion, lethargy, irritability, hyperreflexia, ataxia, seizures, coma.  
- *Key signs:* Dry mucous membranes, decreased skin turgor, tachycardia, hypotension (late).  
- *Action:* Correct slowly with 5% dextrose or free water via NG tube; monitor sodium q2-4h.  
- **Late presentation pattern:** Elderly nursing home resident with vague illness, found to have sodium 165, urine output not measured – common exam case.

**Seizures from rapid osmolar shifts:**  
- If hypernatremia corrected too quickly, water rushes into brain cells causing cerebral oedema → seizures, increased ICP.  
- *Rule:* Use hypotonic fluids at a rate to lower sodium by ≤0.5 mmol/L per hour.

**Chronic bladder enlargement / hydronephrosis** (rare but possible in long-standing severe polyuria if not recognized):  
- High urine volumes cause overdistension → poor bladder contractility, VUR.  
- *Prevention:* Keep urine volume <3 L/day with treatment.

**Desmopressin overdose → hyponatremia:**  
- Water retention without ADH regulation → fluid overload, hyponatremia → cerebral oedema.  
- *Monitor:* Regular sodium checks; educate patient to “skip a dose if you have no thirst or are drinking less than usual.”

**Thirst loss in central diabetes insipidus** (adipsic DI if hypothalamic osmoreceptors damaged):  
- Very dangerous: no thirst drive, severe hypernatremia develops despite polyuria. These patients require scheduled water intake + daily dosing.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonic: DI – "A Dilute Issue"**  
- **A** – ADH deficiency (central) or resistance (nephrogenic)  
- **D** – Dilute urine (low osmolality)  
- **I** – Inappropriate water loss  
- **S** – Sodium high (if inadequate intake) or normal (if drinking enough)

**Mnemonic: Cranial vs Renal – "Central gets DDAVP, Nephrogenic gets Nix"**  
- Central: Desmopressin works.  
- Nephrogenic: Nix the drug (e.g. lithium), Nix the hypercalcemia, Nix hypokalemia.

**5 high-yield pearls:**  
1. **Polyuria + sodium normal** → think primary polydipsia or DI with adequate drinking. Polyuria + sodium high → DI until proven otherwise.  
2. **Post-pituitary surgery: look for triphasic response** – early DI (0-2 days) → SIADH (2-10 days) → permanent DI. Never treat early DI aggressively because the second phase (SIADH) can cause hyponatremia if desmopressin is continued.  
3. **A single urine osmolality > 600 rules out DI** (except if the patient has early diabetes mellitus).  
4. **Hypercalcemia and hypokalemia both cause nephrogenic DI** – always check Ca and K in polyuric patient.  
5. **Desmopressin dose is not weight-based** – start low (1 mcg SC or 100 mcg PO) and titrate to urine output and thirst.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**  
- Mistaking polyuria from DI for diabetes mellitus – check blood glucose.  
- Giving large volumes of normal saline for hypernatremia – it's hypertonic relative to the patient; use hypotonic fluids.  
- Starting water deprivation without checking baseline sodium or clinical hydration status.  
- Forgetting that lithium can cause irreversible nephrogenic DI; early detection stops damage.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**

| Parameter | Threshold | Action |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| **Hypernatremia** | Na > 150 mmol/L (especially if acute) | Urgent senior review; correct slowly; consider ICU if symptoms (seizures, coma) |
| **Rising sodium > 10 mmol/L in 24h** | Rate of correction too rapid or ongoing water loss | Reassess fluid regimen; call senior |
| **Hypotension + tachycardia + poor urine output** | SBP < 90, HR > 120, urine <0.5 ml/kg/hr for 2h | Treat hypovolemia; escalate to ICU (may need central line, vassopressin if refractory) |
| **Seizures or GCS <8** | Any seizure or GCS drop | Crash call, IV lorazepam, urgent sodium; consider cerebral oedema |
| **NEWS2** | Score ≥5 | Escalate to registrar; ≥7 call critical care outreach |
| **Urine output >300 ml/hr for 2 consecutive hours in a patient with head trauma / neurosurgery** | High risk for central DI | Notify neurosurgery; start desmopressin after protocol; monitor sodium hourly |
| **Hyponatremia from desmopressin overdose** | Na <125 with confusion | Stop desmopressin; fluid restriction; consider hypertonic saline if seizures |

**In summary:** Rapidly worsening hypernatremia, neurological symptoms, and hemodynamic instability are red flags. In any polyuric patient with altered consciousness, prioritize checking sodium and starting correct treatment. Build a mental reflex: "Polyuria + high Na = water deprivation test and/or imaging – never just give desmopressin without confirming the type."
